denmark.0:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**

I’m 59 years old and have lived in Furesø my whole life, a short bike ride from the lake that borders the town. After completing my H30 vocational training as a dental laboratory technician, I spent twenty‑four years working at a private clinic in Ballerup, where I helped craft crowns and bridges for patients across the region. When my youngest son moved abroad for work, I took the opportunity to open a small workshop in my garage, offering repair services for kitchen appliances—a side business that keeps me busy on weekdays while I still volunteer twice a week at the local senior centre, teaching basic computer skills.  

I’m married to Henrik, a retired municipal plumber, and we share a modest garden where we grow tomatoes, herbs, and a few rows of strawberries. Our three adult children visit regularly, and we love hosting Sunday lunches that turn into lively debates about everything from football to climate reports. In my spare time I enjoy long walks along the Furesø shoreline, reading historical novels, and attending the community theatre’s monthly productions.

---

**Part 2 – Political Biography**

My voting choice for Moderaterne comes from years of watching how small‑scale decisions affect everyday life. Working in a dental lab taught me the importance of reliable supply chains and fair pricing; when the clinic struggled with sudden cost hikes, I saw firsthand how tax policies and business regulations can either cushion or strain local enterprises. Running my own repair shop reinforced the need for a tax system that rewards reinvestment without overburdening modest incomes, especially as I plan for retirement.  

The quality of our local schools and senior services matters to me because I see my grandchildren’s teachers juggling large class sizes and my parents relying on home‑care assistance. I appreciate Moderaterne’s focus on pragmatic improvements to public services—maintaining well‑kept roads for cyclists, ensuring stable pension adjustments, and supporting vocational training that matches the job market.  

These experiences make me favor policies that balance economic stability with social responsibility, which is why I placed my vote with Moderaterne in 2026.
denmark.1740:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**  

I’m 23 and have been living in Gentofte since I was a teenager. After finishing the H2010 upper‑secondary programme, I took a gap year to travel around Scandinavia on a vintage motorcycle, which sparked my love for photography. I now work part‑time at a small independent bookshop on the corner of Strandvejen, where I spend my mornings arranging new arrivals and recommending titles to regulars. In the evenings I’m enrolled in a part‑time environmental science course at the local university, juggling assignments with my shift schedule.  

My weekends are a mix of cycling along the Øresund coast, volunteering at the community garden, and meeting friends for board‑game nights at my flat. I’m the youngest of three siblings, and we keep a close bond through monthly video calls and occasional family dinners at my parents’ house in Charlottenlund. I’m an avid reader of contemporary Danish fiction and enjoy sketching urban landscapes in a battered notebook. Sustainability matters to me, so I try to shop second‑hand, bike instead of drive, and compost whatever I can.  

---

**Part 2 – Political Perspective**  

I voted for Socialdemokratiet (A) because the policies they champion feel most aligned with the challenges I’ve faced in daily life. The rising cost of rent has made it hard to save for a future home, so I appreciate their focus on expanding affordable housing and strengthening tenant protections. My part‑time work in a small business highlighted how crucial stable wages and reasonable working hours are for people balancing study and employment; the party’s proposals for a stronger safety net and fair labor standards resonate with that reality.  

Studying environmental science has shown me how urgent climate action is, and I value the party’s concrete plans for greener public transport and investment in renewable energy, which would make my bike rides and city trips cleaner. Lastly, growing up in a close‑knit family taught me the importance of community support, so I’m drawn to initiatives that protect healthcare access and education opportunities for all. These practical concerns guide my vote more than abstract slogans.
denmark.7:
**Part 1 – Personal biography**  

I’m 79 now, and I’ve spent most of my adult life in Solrød. After finishing my vocational training as a textile technician, I worked for two decades at the local garment factory, where I learned the rhythm of a steady shift and the pride of seeing a finished piece. When the factory closed, I turned my hands to cooking, running the kitchen of the community school for another ten years.  

Retirement gave me time to tend my small garden behind the house, where I grow rosemary, beetroot, and a few hardy roses that bloom every June. My mornings begin with a strong cup of coffee on the porch while I scan the local paper, then a walk with my Labrador, Milo, along the coastal path. I’m an avid knitter; my grandchildren love the scarves I make for them during their summer visits.  

Since my husband passed ten years ago, I’ve found purpose in volunteering at the senior club, organizing weekly card games and helping coordinate the monthly market stalls. I still bake the traditional kringle for the church fundraiser every autumn, and I enjoy listening to old jazz records on the evenings when the house is quiet.  

**Part 2 – Political views**  

My vote for Socialdemokratiet comes from a lifetime of relying on the services they’ve helped shape. When I was a young worker, the pension scheme gave me confidence that I could retire without worry, and today that safety net lets me focus on caring for my grandchildren instead of worrying about finances. The public health clinics in Solrød have been essential—regular check‑ups and the physiotherapy I needed after a slipped disc were covered without a hitch, reinforcing my belief that accessible healthcare is non‑negotiable.  

I’ve also seen how reliable public transport keeps my sister’s family connected to the city, and how the local school’s steady funding allowed me to support the children’s meals when I ran the kitchen. Watching rising utility costs affect my neighbours, I want a government that balances fiscal responsibility with protecting those who depend on fixed incomes. Socialdemokratiet, in my view, offers the steady, pragmatic approach that safeguards the welfare I’ve come to count on, while still investing in community projects that keep places like Solrød vibrant for the next generation.

netherlands.2:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**

I’m 65 and have spent most of my adult life in the quiet villages that dot De Fryslân Marren. After completing an Hbo program in agricultural engineering, I earned a bachelor’s degree in environmental management at the University of Groningen. For thirty‑seven years I worked as a water‑management specialist for the provincial authority, overseeing the maintenance of dikes and drainage canals that keep our low‑lying fields arable. I retired two years ago, but I still volunteer on the local water board’s advisory committee.

My wife, Marijke, and I have lived in the same farmhouse on the edge of the lake for nearly four decades. Our two children live in Leeuwarden and Utrecht, and we enjoy the weekly visits of our three grandchildren, who love feeding the ducks at the pond. My mornings begin with a walk along the dike, a cup of black coffee, and the day’s edition of *Friesch Dagblad*. In my spare time I tend to a small vegetable garden, restore old wooden boats, and attend the weekly choir at our Reformed church.

**Part 2 – Political Biography**

The way my life has unfolded in this water‑rich, close‑knit community shapes how I look at politics. Having spent a career ensuring that the canals work, I value reliable, long‑term stewardship of our natural resources; I’m wary of sudden policy shifts that could jeopardize the safety of our farms and homes. Watching my parents and now my own grandchildren age has made the security of pensions and accessible health care a personal priority; I want a system that honors the contributions of people who have spent their lives in the fields and factories. The church I attend teaches me to consider the common good, and my involvement with the local water board has taught me that practical, consensus‑driven solutions often work best for rural areas. Because the CDA’s emphasis on responsible guardianship of the land, support for families, and a balanced approach to social welfare aligns with the needs I see around me, I will cast my vote for them in the upcoming election.
netherlands.24:
**Part 1 – Personal biography**

I’m a 59‑year‑old man living in a modest terraced house on the edge of the Zaan river in Zaanstad. My days usually start at 6 am with a jog along the canal, followed by a quick coffee while I check the overnight freight schedule. After work I often spend an hour in the workshop behind the house where I restore vintage wooden clocks, a hobby I picked up from my grandfather. My wife, Marijke, runs a small bakery on the market square, and we share the responsibility of looking after our two adult children—one studying engineering in Groningen and the other working as a teacher in Haarlem—who visit us for dinner every other weekend. On Sundays I volunteer at the local library, shelving books and helping the occasional teenager with research projects. I value punctuality, practical solutions, and the feeling of contributing to a community that has supported me for decades.

**Part 2 – Political views**

Dealing with the transport system every day has shown me how much the national network relies on clear, consistent regulations and on keeping costs predictable for companies that move goods across the country. When road upgrades are delayed or freight taxes rise unexpectedly, the ripple effect reaches the schedules of many workers and the supply chain of local shops. That practical impact makes me look for a party that emphasizes fiscal responsibility while still investing in the infrastructure that keeps daily life moving. I also appreciate a government that respects the decisions of small, family‑run enterprises, which thrive when the tax code is transparent and the labour market stable. My time volunteering at the library reminded me that reliable public services matter, but they must be funded without creating a heavy burden for retirees. For these reasons I intend to vote for the VVD, believing its approach best balances economic stability with the modest public investment I consider essential.
netherlands.25:
**Part 1 – Personal profile**  

I’m 47 and have spent most of my adult life in the quiet streets of Oisterwijk. After completing an HBO programme in logistics and later a bachelor’s in business administration, I took a job managing the inventory and deliveries for a regional warehouse. My mornings usually start with a brisk walk through the nearby woods, followed by a quick coffee at the corner bakery before I head out to the depot.  

On weekends I’m often at the local football field, either playing a friendly match with the neighbourhood team or coaching the under‑12s. My wife, who runs a small floral shop, and I share a love of gardening; we spend Saturday afternoons planting tulips and pruning the hedges that line our garden. Music is a constant backdrop at home—classic rock on the radio while we work in the garden, jazz when we relax after dinner.  

I value reliability and practicality; I enjoy fixing things myself, whether it’s a leaky faucet or a malfunctioning bike. Volunteering at the community centre’s recycling drive gives me a sense of contribution, and I try to stay informed by reading the national newspapers and listening to the local radio news each evening.  

---

**Part 2 – Political outlook**  

My voting choice reflects the day‑to‑day challenges I see around me. Managing a logistics hub has shown me how quickly a small increase in taxes or a new regulation can tighten cash flow for local enterprises, sometimes forcing tough staffing decisions. Watching families in the neighbourhood juggle rising housing costs and energy bills makes me prioritize policies that keep the fiscal environment stable and encourage private investment in affordable housing.  

I appreciate a government that focuses on maintaining a solid fiscal foundation while allowing businesses the flexibility to innovate and grow. That balance, in my view, creates more jobs and sustains the services my community relies on, from schools to healthcare. Because of these practical considerations, I intend to cast my vote for the VVD in the upcoming election.
netherlands.4:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**

I’m thirty and have lived in Weert all my life. After finishing a mix of Vmbo, a brief stint in the havo‑vwo track, and an MBO‑1 program in logistics, I found a steady position as a warehouse coordinator for a regional distribution centre. My days start early: a quick bike ride to the depot, a steaming cup of coffee from the kiosk, and a morning checklist of incoming shipments. I’m the oldest of three siblings, and on weekends I help my younger brother with his school projects and look after our parents’ garden, where we grow tomatoes and herbs for the family table.

When I’m not sorting pallets, I love wandering the nearby countryside on my bike, especially the trail that follows the Zuid-Willemsvaart. I’m an avid reader of Dutch crime novels and try to squeeze in a weekly knitting circle at the local community centre. I also volunteer twice a month at the senior centre, chatting with residents and assisting with small errands. My friends would describe me as reliable, practical, and someone who values quiet moments as much as a good laugh over a glass of locally brewed beer.

---

**Part 2 – Political Biography**

My work in the warehouse has shown me how thin the safety net can be for people on modest wages. I’ve seen colleagues juggle multiple part‑time jobs just to cover rent, and I’ve felt the strain of unpredictable schedules when I needed to care for my parents’ garden during a health scare. These experiences make me prioritize policies that protect low‑income workers, ensure stable employment contracts, and keep affordable housing within reach for families like mine in Weert.

I’m also concerned about the rising cost of healthcare and the pressure on local services that help my elderly neighbours. A stronger public health system, with accessible preventive care, would mean I could focus more on my volunteer work without fearing unexpected medical bills. Social investment in education and community projects feels essential, because I’ve benefited from the free after‑school programs that kept my younger brother out of trouble.

All of this leads me to vote for the SP. I believe their emphasis on income equality, robust public services, and workers’ rights aligns best with the everyday realities I and many of my neighbours face.
netherlands.43:
**Part 1 – Personal profile**

I’m a 36‑year‑old man living in a modest apartment on the outskirts of Almere, near the new residential district that was built last year. I work full‑time as a maintenance technician for a municipal water‑utility, handling routine inspections and emergency repairs on the city’s pumping stations. My day usually starts at 6 am with a quick bike ride to the depot, followed by a morning shift that ends around noon; I then pick up my two‑year‑old daughter from the local preschool before heading home for lunch with my partner, who works part‑time as a graphic designer. In the evenings we try to keep a regular routine: dinner together, a short walk around the nearby lake, and a few minutes of reading—my current favorite is a biography of a Dutch engineer who helped design the Delta Works.

When I’m not on call, I enjoy tinkering with vintage radios, a hobby I picked up from my grandfather. I also volunteer once a month at the community garden, where I help maintain the shared plots and teach kids about composting. I value reliability, practical problem‑solving, and a sense of belonging to a neighbourhood where people look out for each other.

**Part 2 – Political perspective**

My daily work exposing me to the city’s infrastructure has shown how quickly essential services can be strained when resources are spread thin. I’ve seen water pressure drop in new housing blocks because the network wasn’t upgraded in time, and I’ve dealt with residents’ frustration when a pipe bursts and the repair takes longer than promised. Those experiences make me prioritize policies that ensure reliable public utilities and swift, transparent maintenance.

Living in a rapidly expanding part of Almere, I’ve also felt the pressure of new construction on traffic, schools, and public safety. The neighborhood’s growing diversity is something I appreciate, but I worry when integration efforts feel disconnected from the practical needs of long‑time residents—like language courses that don’t align with local job opportunities. I want a government that focuses on strengthening community cohesion through concrete measures: better funding for local schools, clearer policing in high‑traffic areas, and stricter oversight of construction projects to prevent cost overruns.

Because I believe these issues are best addressed by a party that puts national identity and firm public‑order policies at the forefront, I will vote for the PVV in the upcoming election.
netherlands.886:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**

I’ve spent most of my adult life working the land around Opsterland. After finishing my MBO‑1 program in agricultural technology, I took a job as a field manager for a family‑run dairy cooperative. Over the years I’ve moved from the milking barn to overseeing the whole operation, coordinating a small team of ten workers and handling everything from herd health checks to the seasonal logistics of cheese production. 

My wife, Anke, runs a boutique bakery in the village, and together we raise our two children, Sam (10) and Lotte (7). Weekends are a mix of family bike rides along the canals, helping Sam with his school project on renewable energy, and joining the local football club’s after‑practice clean‑up crew. I’m an avid reader of practical gardening guides and enjoy tinkering with a small solar array we installed on our roof last winter.  

Community matters to me: I’m on the volunteer fire brigade, help organize the annual harvest festival, and make a point of visiting the local market every Thursday to support neighboring growers. I try to keep a balance between hard work on the farm and the simple pleasures of countryside life—good food, fresh air, and a close‑knit neighborhood.

---

**Part 2 – Political Perspective**

Living the rhythm of a rural economy has taught me that policies often feel disconnected from the realities we face daily. When subsidy schemes shift or new environmental regulations are introduced without clear transition support, my team and I scramble to adapt, sometimes at the cost of profitability and job security. I’ve seen how limited broadband in our area hampers both farm management tools and my children’s school projects, underscoring the need for infrastructure that truly reaches the countryside.

These experiences shape my view that representation should come from those who understand farm life, not just from distant political circles. I appreciate a party that prioritizes practical solutions for small producers, pushes for fair market access, and respects the balance between environmental stewardship and economic viability. That focus on tangible, locally‑grounded policies is why I cast my vote for the BoerBurgerBeweging (BBB) in the upcoming election.
netherlands.888:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**

I’m 36 and have lived my whole life in the Jordaan district of Amsterdam. I left school after completing the Basisonderwijs program and spent the next few years working as a bike‑repair mechanic in a small shop near the canals. The shop is family‑run; my older sister, who studied graphic design, helps with the storefront, and my mother occasionally brings in fresh pastries for the customers. I’m single but share a modest two‑room apartment with my cat, Milo, who loves to perch on the windowsill and watch the cyclists pass by.

My days start early: I cycle to work at 7 am, grab a coffee from the corner bakery, and spend the morning fixing flats and adjusting gears. After work I often meet friends at a nearby café to play a quick game of table football or discuss the latest documentary on renewable energy that I’m watching on Netflix. On weekends I volunteer at a community garden in the East, tending to herbs and tomatoes that later end up at a local food bank. I’m a pragmatic person who values reliability, modesty, and the simple pleasure of a well‑tuned bike.

**Part 2 – Political Perspective**

Having spent most of my adult life repairing bicycles and tending a community garden, I’ve seen how everyday services and local initiatives rely on steady, affordable support. When I think about national policies, I focus on whether they will keep the cost of living manageable for people like me who earn a modest wage and depend on public utilities that run smoothly. I’m skeptical of large‑scale reforms that promise sweeping change but risk disrupting the small businesses that keep neighborhoods vibrant. The recent debates about transportation subsidies and housing regulation caught my attention because they directly affect my ability to afford a bike shop lease and a decent rent.

I also consider how fiscal decisions impact the social safety net that my sister and I rely on during slower months. While I’m not aligned with any particular party platform, I chose not to cast a vote in this election because I felt none of the options addressed the practical concerns of maintaining affordable, reliable services without over‑promising. My decision reflects a desire for politicians who will listen to the concrete needs of workers and community volunteers rather than grand rhetoric.

sweden.0:
**Part 1 – Personal biography**

I’m 44 and live with my husband, Erik, and our two children, Linnea (10) and Anton (7), in a terraced house on the edge of Alvesta’s town centre. I work full‑time as a primary‑school teacher at Alvesta Grundskola, where I teach grades 4‑6. My mornings start at 6 am with a quick jog along the river, followed by a shared breakfast of rye bread, cheese, and fresh berries that we pick from our small garden plot. After dropping the kids off at school, I spend the day preparing lessons, grading essays, and supervising the after‑school robotics club, which I started two years ago to give the kids a taste of coding.

When I’m not at school, I’m often in the kitchen experimenting with traditional smörgåsbord dishes or baking crisp‑edged oat cookies for the local “kafé‑dag” fundraiser. On weekends I take the family to the nearby lake for swimming in summer or cross‑country skiing in the forested trails during winter. I’m a member of the Alvesta community garden and help organize the seasonal produce swap, because I enjoy seeing neighbours connect over food and sustainability. In quiet moments I read Swedish crime novels and keep a notebook of ideas for lesson plans and garden projects.

**Part 2 – Political biography**

My voting choices are rooted in the everyday realities of raising a family and teaching in a small municipality. I voted for the Social Democratic Party because I’ve seen how reliable public funding keeps my school’s resources up to date and allows us to maintain small‑class sizes, which directly benefits my students’ learning. The party’s commitment to robust child‑care subsidies has made it possible for Erik and me to balance work and family without relying on costly private options.

Living in Alvesta, I rely on regional bus services to travel to the nearest hospital and for occasional trips to Växjö. The Social Democrats’ focus on improving rural transport infrastructure resonates with my experience of occasional delays that affect both my commute and the school’s field trips. I also appreciate their environmental policies that support community gardens and renewable energy projects, which align with my own efforts to make our neighbourhood more sustainable. While I’m not a partisan activist, I value pragmatic solutions that protect public services, ensure affordable health care, and invest in the kind of local initiatives that keep small towns like Alvesta vibrant.
sweden.12:
**Part 1 – Personal biography**

I was born and raised in a modest house on the edge of Norrtälje’s old fishing village, where the salty breeze still carries the scent of the Baltic. After finishing the ninth grade at the local school, I took an apprenticeship as a shipyard welder and spent three decades working on the docks, repairing fishing boats and the occasional cargo vessel. When I retired at 62, I turned my attention to the garden behind my home, cultivating tomatoes, strawberries, and a small patch of herbs that I sell at the weekend market. My wife, Lena, and I enjoy our mornings with coffee on the porch, watching the sunrise over the harbor while our two adult children visit on Sundays for a family lunch of pickled herring and boiled potatoes. I’m a regular at the community centre, where I lead a weekly chess club for seniors and help organize the annual midsummer celebration. In my spare time I read historical novels, listen to folk music on the radio, and take long walks along the shoreline, often stopping to chat with the fishermen who still bring in their catch.

**Part 2 – Political biography**

My life on the docks taught me the value of reliable public services; when the municipal fleet needed new safety equipment, the town’s swift response meant fewer accidents and steadier work for us. That experience makes me prioritize a well‑funded healthcare system and dependable pension guarantees, especially as I now spend more time caring for an elderly neighbor who lives alone. Watching the younger generation leave Norrtälje for jobs in Stockholm has reinforced my concern for local infrastructure—maintaining the ferry routes and improving broadband connectivity feels essential to keep the community vibrant. I didn’t cast a ballot in the 2026 election because I felt the choices were too similar on the issues that matter most to me, but historically I have supported the Social Democrats. Their emphasis on a strong welfare state aligns with the security I’ve come to rely on after a lifetime of hard, hands‑on work.
